text: The surgeon begins by incising the mesocolon at the junction with the small bowel mesentery. Next, the peritoneum is incised to expose the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) along the midline.
The surgeon cuts the vascular sheath, revealing a clear space between the sheath and the artery. This space is free of small vessels, allowing for safe operation without risk of vessel injury.

The surgeon isolates the artery and vessel, carefully turning the working shell of the harmonic scalpel outward to prevent vessel damage. The surgical field is expanded, revealing the surrounding structures and opposing the culture of the SMOE.

The surgeon exposes the ileocolic vein and vessel, dissecting them in preparation for removal. The central lymph node is excised en bloc along with the mesocolon. Small vessel branches from the superior mesenteric vein are visible during the dissection.
The surgeon carefully exposes the area of the colon, avoiding bleeding while clipping and translating the colon structures.
The surgeon identifies and clears lymph nodes numbered two, yellow, and three, while continuing to expand the surgical space within the mesocolon.
The surgeon navigates the right colon and enters the fascial plane anterior to the pancreas, expanding the surgical space.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the number fourteen lymph node, ensuring it is removed en bloc with the muscle column. The area is approached with caution to prevent bleeding and avoid injury to nearby vessels and lymphatic tubes.
The surgeon identifies the overlap of the surgical lesion, allowing for easy clamping and tracing of the vessel branches.
The surgeon has resected the central liver node and continues to expand the surgical space, revealing a smooth vessel within the operative field.
The surgeon returns to the anterior aspect over the pancreas, expanding the surgical field laterally to the descending colon and inferiorly to the omentum.

The surgeon cuts the omentum towards the spleen and hepatic flexure near the gastric block, dissecting the number C lymph node. The transverse mesocolon is cut at its root near the pancreas, and the SMA lymph node is dissected, continuing to cut the mesocolon at its root towards the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon identifies the space between the mesentery and the liver, referred to as the 'tree junction,' and prepares to cut using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon completes the resection of the right colon, revealing the smooth overlap of the operation region. The superior mesenteric artery (SMA), duodenal, pancreatic head, and fissure are visible. A side-to-side conjunction is performed.
